intro.overview: Welcome to NEXUS Security Consulting. In this foundational lab, you will deploy your personal security operations platform using Kali Linux in a virtualized environment. This setup will serve as your primary workspace for all hands-on exercises throughout the semester. You'll learn to configure virtual machines, install Docker for containerized client targets, and verify your environment is ready for purple team operations.

mentorQuote.title: Senior Penetration Tester, NEXUS Security
mentorQuote.quote: Every analyst at NEXUS starts here — building their own lab environment. This isn't just homework; it's your personal security workstation. Treat it like your toolkit. You'll use this setup for every client engagement this semester. Document everything — your future self will thank you when you're writing runbooks at 2 AM.

focusAreas: Kali Linux as a purpose-built security testing platform
Docker container fundamentals and container vs. VM differences
objectives: Deploy Kali Linux as your security operations platform
Install and verify Docker for containerized client targets
Document your environment for your Lab 1.3 Engagement Packet

callouts.title: Save For Lab 1.3
callouts.text: This is Week 1 of a 3-week module. Create a folder called Module1_Screenshots and save EVERY screenshot there. You'll compile these into your Lab 1.3 Engagement Packet in Week 3.

timeGuide.timesheetNote: This is Week 1 of 3. Log these hours as Internal Training on your Lab 1.3 timesheet. Save every screenshot to your Module1_Screenshots folder — you’ll need them all in Week 3.

content.whatYoureDoing: Installing VirtualBox, a free hypervisor that allows you to run virtual machines on your computer.
content.whyItMatters: Virtual machines allow you to run a complete operating system inside your existing OS. This is how security professionals maintain isolated environments for testing tools, malware analysis, and client engagements without risking their primary system.

steps.text: Navigate to virtualbox.org/wiki/Downloads
Download the installer for your host operating system (Windows, macOS, or Linux)
Run the installer with default settings
Restart your computer if prompted
Launch VirtualBox to verify installation

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: macOS users: You may need to allow the kernel extension in System Preferences > Security & Privacy
Windows users: Enable Hyper-V/virtualization in BIOS if VirtualBox won't start VMs

content.title: What You Should Have
content.body: VirtualBox Manager window opens successfully, showing an empty list of virtual machines.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.body: Chapter 2 introduces defense in depth and the layered security model. Virtual machines provide isolation — a core security control that prevents testing activities from affecting production systems. Reading this chapter? That's a reference for your Lab 1.3 report.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.body: What's the difference between Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisors? Research where VirtualBox falls in this classification and why it matters for security testing.

content.title: That's a Reference!
content.body: VirtualBox User Manual, Chapter 1: Introduction. Oracle Corporation. Available at: https://www.virtualbox.org/manual/

content.whatYoureDoing: Downloading the Kali Linux installer image (~4GB) from the official source.
content.whyItMatters: Kali Linux is a Debian-based distribution designed specifically for penetration testing and security auditing. It comes pre-loaded with hundreds of security tools that you'll use throughout the semester for both offensive and defensive work.

steps.text: Navigate to kali.org/get-kali
Select Installer Images
Choose the correct architecture: 64-bit (amd64): For Intel/AMD processors Apple Silicon (arm64): For M1/M2/M3 Macs using UTM
Download the ISO file (approximately 4GB)
Note the download location — you'll need this for the next checkpoint

content.title: Time-Saving Tip
content.body: Start this download before working on other tasks. A 4GB download can take 10-30 minutes depending on your connection. You can proceed to review the remaining checkpoints while it downloads.

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Wrong architecture: Download the amd64 (64-bit) version unless you have Apple Silicon
Unofficial sources: Only download from kali.org — third-party ISOs may contain malware
Interrupted download: If download fails, delete partial file and restart — don't resume corrupted downloads

content.title: What You Should Have
content.body: A file named something like kali-linux-2024.x-installer-amd64.iso in your Downloads folder.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.body: Chapter 1 discusses security tools and the importance of using trusted sources. Kali Linux is purpose-built for security testing, containing tools you'll use throughout this course for both offensive and defensive work.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.body: Why is Kali Linux based on Debian rather than another distribution? Research the advantages of Debian's package management for security tools.

content.title: That's a Reference!
content.body: Kali Linux Documentation - What is Kali Linux? Offensive Security. Available at: https://www.kali.org/docs/introduction/what-is-kali-linux/

content.whatYoureDoing: Creating a new virtual machine in VirtualBox with settings optimized for Kali Linux.
content.whyItMatters: Proper VM configuration affects performance, stability, and functionality. The VM name is especially important — it appears in your terminal prompt and must be visible in screenshots for assignment verification.

steps.text: In VirtualBox Manager, click New
Configure the VM name using this exact format: kali-lastname-studentID Example: kali-newsom-abc123
Set Type: Linux, Version: Debian (64-bit)
Allocate RAM: 4096 MB minimum (8192 MB recommended if available)
Create a virtual hard disk: VDI format, Dynamically allocated, 80 GB
Before starting, go to Settings > System > Processor: Allocate 2+ CPUs
Go to Settings > Storage: Click the empty disk under Controller: IDE, then click the disk icon and choose your Kali ISO

content.title: Critical: VM Naming Convention
content.body: Your VM name must follow the format kali-lastname-studentID. This name appears in your terminal prompt and verifies that screenshots are your own work. Incorrect naming will result in unverifiable screenshots.

content.title: Screenshot
content.body: Capture your VM settings window showing the VM name, RAM allocation, and processor count. Save as 01-vm-settings.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Wrong VM name format: Must be kali-lastname-studentID exactly — this appears in all your screenshots
Too little RAM: 2GB minimum, 4GB recommended — less causes performance issues
ISO not attached: VM won't boot if the ISO isn't mounted in the optical drive settings

content.title: What You Should Have
content.body: A VM entry in VirtualBox Manager with your name following the kali-lastname-studentID format, with the Kali ISO attached.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.body: Chapter 2 covers system resources and security considerations. Proper resource allocation ensures your testing environment performs reliably without impacting your host system's stability.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.body: What are VDI, VMDK, and VHD disk formats? Research the differences and why VDI is preferred for VirtualBox.

content.title: That's a Reference!
content.body: VirtualBox User Manual, Chapter 5: Virtual Storage. Oracle Corporation. Available at: https://www.virtualbox.org/manual/ch05.html

content.whatYoureDoing: Running the Kali Linux installer inside your virtual machine to create a functional security testing environment.
content.whyItMatters: Kali Linux comes pre-loaded with hundreds of security tools. A proper installation ensures all tools work correctly and the system is stable for the entire semester.

steps.text: Start the VM in VirtualBox Manager
Select Graphical Install from the boot menu
Choose your language, location, and keyboard layout
Set hostname to match your VM name: kali-lastname-studentID
Leave domain name blank
Create your user account (remember these credentials!)
Use Guided - use entire disk for partitioning
Select All files in one partition
Confirm and write changes to disk
Wait for installation to complete (15-25 minutes)
Reboot when prompted

content.title: Screenshot
content.body: Capture the Kali desktop after login, with the terminal open showing your hostname. Save as 02-kali-desktop.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Forgetting credentials: Write down your username and password — you'll need them all semester
Wrong hostname: The hostname should match your VM name format
Installation hangs: If stuck, check that VT-x/AMD-V is enabled in BIOS

content.title: What You Should Have
content.body: A fully installed Kali Linux system that boots to a graphical desktop. You should be able to log in with your username and password.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.body: Chapter 2 discusses operating system security and hardening. Understanding the installation process helps you make informed security decisions about system configuration.

content.whatYoureDoing: Installing Docker, the containerization platform we'll use to deploy vulnerable lab targets throughout the course.
content.whyItMatters: Docker lets us spin up isolated, vulnerable systems safely. Each module's client engagement uses Docker containers — without it, you can't complete the labs.

steps.text: Update your package lists: sudo apt update ▶ What this does Concept: apt update refreshes the list of available packages from the repositories. It does not install anything — it just checks what’s available. Always run this before installing new software so you get the latest versions.

whatThisDoes.concept: apt update refreshes the list of available packages from the repositories. It does not install anything — it just checks what's available. Always run this before installing new software so you get the latest versions.

steps.text: Install Docker and Docker Compose: sudo apt install -y docker.io docker-compose ▶ What this does Concept: apt install downloads and installs packages. The -y flag automatically answers “yes” to the confirmation prompt. docker.io is the container engine; docker-compose lets you define multi-container environments in a single file. We’ll use both throughout the course.

whatThisDoes.concept: apt install downloads and installs packages. The -y flag automatically answers "yes" to the confirmation prompt. docker.io is the container engine; docker-compose lets you define multi-container environments in a single file.

steps.text: Enable Docker to start automatically and start it now: sudo systemctl enable docker --now ▶ What this does Concept: systemctl enable tells the system to start Docker automatically on every boot. The --now flag also starts it immediately so you don’t have to reboot first.

whatThisDoes.concept: systemctl enable tells the system to start Docker automatically on every boot. The --now flag also starts it immediately so you don't have to reboot first.

steps.text: Add your user to the Docker group: sudo usermod -aG docker $USER ▶ What this does Concept: usermod -aG appends (-a) your user to a group (-G). By default, Docker commands require root access. Adding yourself to the docker group lets you run Docker without sudo every time.

whatThisDoes.concept: usermod -aG appends your user to a group. By default, Docker commands require root access. Adding yourself to the docker group lets you run Docker without sudo every time.

content.title: Pro Tip
content.body: After adding yourself to the docker group, you must log out and back in (or reboot) for the group membership to take effect. Until then, you'll need sudo for docker commands.

steps.text: Check the Docker version: docker --version ▶ What this does In your output: You should see the installed Docker version number. If you get “command not found” or a permission error, the installation didn’t complete correctly or you haven’t logged out and back in yet.

whatThisDoes.output: You should see the installed Docker version number. If you get "command not found" or a permission error, the installation didn't complete correctly or you haven't logged out and back in yet.

steps.text: Run the Docker test container: docker run hello-world ▶ What this does Concept: docker run pulls an image from Docker Hub and runs it as a container. The hello-world image is a tiny test container that prints a success message. If you see “Hello from Docker!” — Docker is fully working.

whatThisDoes.concept: docker run pulls an image from Docker Hub and runs it as a container. The hello-world image is a tiny test container that prints a success message. If you see "Hello from Docker!" — Docker is fully working.

content.title: Screenshot
content.body: Capture docker --version and the hello-world output. Save as 03-docker-version.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Permission denied: You didn't log out after adding yourself to the docker group
Cannot connect to daemon: Docker service isn't running — check with sudo systemctl status docker
Network errors: VM needs internet access — check VirtualBox network settings

content.title: What You Should Have
content.body: Docker installed and running. The hello-world container should display "Hello from Docker!" confirming everything works.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.body: What's the difference between a container and a virtual machine? Research how Docker's architecture differs from VirtualBox and why containers are preferred for deploying applications.

content.title: That's a Reference!
content.body: Docker Documentation: Get Docker. Available at: https://docs.docker.com/get-docker/

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.body: Chapter 2 covers defense in depth and isolation as a security control. Docker uses Linux kernel features — namespaces and cgroups — to create lightweight isolation without a full hypervisor. Containers and VMs represent two different points on the isolation spectrum that security professionals choose between based on their threat model.

content.whatYoureDoing: Running a series of commands to verify your environment is properly configured and all required tools are available.
content.whyItMatters: Catching configuration issues now prevents frustration later. A working baseline environment means you can focus on learning security concepts instead of troubleshooting setup problems.

steps.text: Display your hostname: hostname ▶ What this does In your output: This should show kali-lastname-studentID (the hostname you set in Checkpoint 4). If it shows something else, you need to fix it before proceeding.

whatThisDoes.output: This should show kali-lastname-studentID. If it shows something else, you need to fix it before proceeding.

steps.text: Display kernel and OS information: uname -a ▶ What this does Concept: uname -a prints all system information: kernel name, hostname, kernel version, architecture. The -a flag means “all.” This is a standard first step when documenting an engagement environment.

whatThisDoes.concept: uname -a prints all system information: kernel name, hostname, kernel version, architecture. The -a flag means "all." This is a standard first step when documenting an engagement environment.

steps.text: Display your network interfaces and IP addresses: ip addr ▶ What this does Concept: ip addr lists all network interfaces and their assigned IP addresses. Look for your eth0 interface — it should have an IP address assigned by VirtualBox’s NAT adapter (typically 10.0.2.x). No IP means no network connectivity.

whatThisDoes.concept: ip addr lists all network interfaces and their assigned IP addresses. Look for your eth0 interface — it should have an IP address assigned by VirtualBox's NAT adapter (typically 10.0.2.x).

steps.text: Test internet connectivity: ping -c 3 8.8.8.8 ▶ What this does Concept: ping sends ICMP echo requests to test network reachability. The -c 3 flag limits it to 3 packets (otherwise it runs forever). 8.8.8.8 is Google’s public DNS. If all 3 packets come back, you have internet connectivity.

whatThisDoes.concept: ping sends ICMP echo requests to test network reachability. The -c 3 flag limits it to 3 packets. 8.8.8.8 is Google's public DNS. If all 3 packets come back, you have internet connectivity.

steps.text: Verify Nmap is installed: nmap --version ▶ What this does Concept: Nmap is the industry-standard network scanner. You’ll use it heavily starting in Module 1 Lab 2. The --version flag confirms it’s installed and shows the version number.

whatThisDoes.concept: Nmap is the industry-standard network scanner. You'll use it heavily starting in Module 1 Lab 2. The --version flag confirms it's installed and shows the version number.

steps.text: Verify Wireshark is installed: wireshark --version ▶ What this does Concept: Wireshark is a packet capture and analysis tool. You’ll use it in Module 3 to inspect network traffic. Confirming the version now ensures it’s ready when you need it.

whatThisDoes.concept: Wireshark is a packet capture and analysis tool. You'll use it in Module 3 to inspect network traffic.

steps.text: Verify John the Ripper is installed: john --help | head -5 ▶ What this does Concept: John the Ripper is a password cracking tool you’ll use in Module 4. The | head -5 pipe takes the first 5 lines of output, which is enough to confirm it’s installed without flooding your terminal.

whatThisDoes.concept: John the Ripper is a password cracking tool you'll use in Module 4. The | head -5 pipe takes the first 5 lines of output — enough to confirm it's installed without flooding your terminal.

steps.text: Confirm Docker version: docker --version

Verify no containers are running yet: docker ps ▶ What this does Concept: docker ps lists running containers. Right now it should show an empty table (just headers). In later modules, this is how you’ll confirm your lab target containers are running before you start scanning.

whatThisDoes.concept: docker ps lists running containers. Right now it should show an empty table (just headers). In later modules, this is how you'll confirm your lab target containers are running before you start scanning.

content.title: Screenshot
content.body: Capture a terminal showing output from the verification commands. Your hostname must be visible. Save as 04-system-verification.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: No network: Check VirtualBox network adapter settings (NAT should work)
Tool not found: Some tools may need installation — use sudo apt install <tool-name>
Wrong hostname: If hostname doesn't match expected format, ask in Discord before proceeding

content.title: What You Should Have
content.body: All verification commands running successfully. Your Kali environment is ready for the semester's security labs and exercises.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.body: Chapter 2 introduces the security tools and platforms you'll use throughout the course. Your verified environment is the foundation for all hands-on exercises.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.body: Explore the Kali Linux tools menu. How many categories of tools are available? Pick one category and research what 2-3 of its tools do.

items.label: Isolated Attack Platforms
items.text: Virtual machines let attackers operate from a clean, disposable environment. After an engagement, the VM can be rolled back or deleted — leaving no artifacts on the analyst’s host.

items.label: Container Recon Targets
items.text: Docker containers are everywhere in modern infrastructure. Understanding how they run and expose ports is essential for finding attack surface in containerized environments.

items.label: Tool Availability
items.text: Kali Linux pre-loads every tool you’ll use this semester. Attackers maintain their own customized distributions — having a ready toolkit is a force multiplier.

items.label: Environment Hygiene
items.text: Defenders use virtualization the same way — isolated sandboxes for malware analysis, forensics, and safe tool testing. Understanding the tech from both sides makes you a better analyst.

items.label: Container Security Posture
items.text: Every port mapped in docker run is a potential attack surface. Defenders must understand container networking to audit what’s actually exposed.

items.label: Documentation Discipline
items.text: Blue teamers live and die by documentation. Building the habit of capturing and naming screenshots in Week 1 reflects how real SOC analysts maintain evidence chains.

purpleTeam.connection: Your lab environment is both your attack platform and your first blue team lesson. Every security professional — red or blue — maintains an isolated, documented, reproducible workspace. This setup is that workspace.

sections.title: Environment Verification
items: VM named exactly kali-lastname-studentID
Kali Linux boots to desktop
Docker installed, running, and hello-world container executes

needHelp.nextModule: Lab 1.2: Reconnaissance Tool Exploration — You'll use your new Kali environment to run your first security scans against the Brazos Financial training targets.
needHelp.lateWorkPolicy: Late work is not accepted. Drop policies exist for emergencies.

permitted: Explain command syntax and tool functionality
prohibited: Complete hands-on exercises on your behalf
genAI.principle: AI is a tutor, not a ghostwriter.
